annotations.label: Velocity

anchor.snippet: Velocity is a measurement of speed in a certain direction of motion

annotations.note: Velocity is a physics concept; Mathlib has no notion of velocity, only the underlying derivative machinery.

annotations.label: Speed

anchor.snippet: The scalar absolute value ( magnitude ) of velocity is called speed

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes vector magnitude (`norm`) but not the physics naming of speed as the magnitude of velocity.

annotations.label: Acceleration

anchor.snippet: If there is a change in speed, direction or both, then the object is said to be undergoing an acceleration

annotations.note: Acceleration is a physics concept not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Average velocity

anchor.snippet: The average velocity of an object over a period of time is its change in position

annotations.note: This physics definition (displacement over elapsed time) has no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: The average velocity of an object over a period of time is its change in position

annotations.label: Instantaneous velocity

anchor.snippet: The instantaneous velocity of an object is the limit average velocity as the time interval approaches zero

annotations.note: The limit-of-difference-quotient idea is exactly Mathlib's `deriv`, but velocity-of-position is not formalized as such.

anchors.snippet: The instantaneous velocity of an object is the limit average velocity as the time interval approaches zero

annotations.label: Area under v–t graph is displacement

anchor.snippet: the area under a velocity vs. time ( v vs. t graph) is the displacement

annotations.note: This is an instance of the fundamental theorem of calculus, formalized generally as `integral_eq_sub_of_hasDerivAt`.

annotations.label: Speed vs velocity

anchor.snippet: Speed, the scalar magnitude of a velocity vector, denotes only how fast an object is moving

annotations.note: The conceptual scalar-vs-vector distinction for speed/velocity is physics terminology, not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Condition for constant velocity

anchor.snippet: To have a constant velocity , an object must have a constant speed in a constant direction

annotations.note: A physics characterization of constant velocity with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Car in circular path

anchor.snippet: a car moving at a constant 20 kilometres per hour in a circular path has a constant speed

annotations.note: A physics illustrative example, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Units of velocity

anchor.snippet: velocity is measured in metres per second

annotations.note: Physical units are not modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Velocity as rate of change of position

anchor.snippet: Velocity is defined as the rate of change of position with respect to time

annotations.note: The rate-of-change concept is `deriv`, but its identification with velocity is physics-specific and not formalized.

annotations.label: Average velocity ≤ average speed

anchor.snippet: The average velocity is always less than or equal to the average speed of an object

annotations.note: Equivalent to ‖∫v‖ ≤ ∫‖v‖, the triangle inequality for integrals formalized as `norm_integral_le_integral_norm`.

annotations.label: Velocity as slope of tangent line

anchor.snippet: the instantaneous velocity (or, simply, velocity) can be thought of as the slope of the tangent line to the curve at any point

annotations.note: The derivative-as-slope-of-tangent interpretation is captured by `HasDerivAt`/`deriv`, though not phrased in terms of velocity.

annotations.label: Average speed over equal time intervals

anchor.snippet: When a particle moves with different uniform speeds

annotations.note: A physics word-problem setup with no corresponding Mathlib theorem.

anchors.snippet: When a particle moves with different uniform speeds

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean of speeds

anchor.snippet: average speed is given by the arithmetic mean of the speeds

annotations.note: Mathlib has Finset averages but no named theorem equating average speed over equal times with an arithmetic mean.

anchors.snippet: average speed is given by the arithmetic mean of the speeds

annotations.label: Average speed over equal distances

anchor.snippet: When a particle moves different distances

annotations.note: A physics word-problem setup not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: When a particle moves different distances

annotations.label: Harmonic mean of speeds

anchor.snippet: average speed is given by the harmonic mean of the speeds

annotations.note: Harmonic mean appears only in inequality lemmas (MeanInequalities) and is not defined as a function nor linked to average speed.

anchors.snippet: average speed is given by the harmonic mean of the speeds

annotations.label: Instantaneous acceleration

anchor.snippet: instantaneous acceleration is defined as the derivative of velocity with respect to time

annotations.note: The derivative operation `deriv` exists but acceleration as the derivative of velocity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Velocity as area under a–t graph

anchor.snippet: velocity is expressed as the area under an a ( t ) acceleration vs. time graph

annotations.note: Another instance of the fundamental theorem of calculus, formalized as `integral_eq_sub_of_hasDerivAt`.

annotations.label: suvat velocity equation

anchor.snippet: In the special case of constant acceleration, velocity can be studied using the suvat equations

annotations.note: The kinematic suvat equations are physics results not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: In the special case of constant acceleration, velocity can be studied using the suvat equations

annotations.label: Displacement and average velocity

anchor.snippet: it is possible to relate the displacement and the average velocity

annotations.note: A kinematics relation under constant acceleration, not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: it is possible to relate the displacement and the average velocity

annotations.label: Torricelli equation

anchor.snippet: known as the Torricelli equation

annotations.note: The Torricelli kinematic equation (v²=u²+2as) is a physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Momentum

anchor.snippet: Newton's second law defines momentum , p, as a vector that is the product of an object's mass and velocity

annotations.note: Momentum is a physics quantity not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kinetic energy

anchor.snippet: The kinetic energy of a moving object is dependent on its velocity

annotations.note: Kinetic energy is a physics concept with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Drag force

anchor.snippet: drag is a force acting opposite to the relative motion of any object moving with respect to a surrounding fluid

annotations.note: Drag is a fluid-dynamics concept not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Drag force equation

anchor.snippet: is dependent on the square of velocity and is given as

annotations.note: The drag-force formula is an empirical physics equation absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Escape velocity

anchor.snippet: Escape velocity is the minimum speed a ballistic object needs to escape from a massive body such as Earth

annotations.note: Escape velocity is a gravitational-physics concept not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Escape velocity is the minimum speed a ballistic object needs to escape from a massive body such as Earth

annotations.label: Lorentz factor

anchor.section: The Lorentz factor of special relativity
anchor.snippet: the dimensionless Lorentz factor appears frequently, and is given by

annotations.note: A grep for `Lorentz` found no relevant declaration; the special-relativity Lorentz factor is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relative velocity

anchor.snippet: Relative velocity is a measurement of velocity between two objects as determined in a single coordinate system

annotations.note: Relative velocity is a physics concept not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relative velocity as vector difference

anchor.snippet: the velocity of object A relative to object B is defined as the difference of the two velocity vectors

annotations.note: This reduces to vector subtraction (`Sub`), but no Mathlib decl formalizes the physics notion of relative velocity.

anchors.snippet: the velocity of object A relative to object B is defined as the difference of the two velocity vectors

annotations.label: One-dimensional relative velocity

anchor.snippet: the velocities are scalars and the equation is either

annotations.note: Scalar relative-velocity addition/subtraction is trivial arithmetic, not a formalized physics statement.

anchors.snippet: the velocities are scalars and the equation is either

annotations.label: Cartesian velocity components

anchor.snippet: velocity is broken up into components that correspond with each dimensional axis

annotations.note: Mathlib has coordinate functions on `EuclideanSpace`, but Cartesian velocity components as such are not formalized.

annotations.label: Speed from 2D distance formula

anchor.snippet: The magnitude of this vector represents speed and is found by the distance formula

annotations.note: The Euclidean magnitude/distance formula ‖x‖ = √(∑ xᵢ²) is formalized generally as `EuclideanSpace.norm_eq`.

annotations.label: Three-dimensional velocity component

anchor.snippet: In three-dimensional systems where there is an additional z-axis, the corresponding velocity component is defined as

annotations.note: A physics component definition with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Radial and transverse velocity

anchor.snippet: a two-dimensional velocity is described by a radial velocity

annotations.note: Radial/transverse velocity decomposition is a physics concept not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angular velocity

anchor.snippet: Both arise from angular velocity , which is the rate of rotation about the origin

annotations.note: Angular velocity is a physics concept with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Transverse velocity

anchor.snippet: The transverse velocity is the component of velocity along a circle centered at the origin

annotations.note: Transverse velocity is a physics concept not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The transverse velocity is the component of velocity along a circle centered at the origin

annotations.label: Radial speed

anchor.snippet: The radial speed (or magnitude of the radial velocity) is the dot product of the velocity vector and the unit vector in the radial direction

annotations.note: The dot product underlying radial speed is formalized (`Matrix.dotProduct`/inner product), but the physics statement is not.

anchors.snippet: The radial speed (or magnitude of the radial velocity) is the dot product of the velocity vector and the unit vector in the radial direction

annotations.label: Transverse speed

anchor.snippet: The transverse speed (or magnitude of the transverse velocity) is the magnitude of the cross product of the unit vector in the radial direction and the velocity vector

annotations.note: The cross product is formalized as `crossProduct`, but its use to define transverse speed is not.

anchors.snippet: The transverse speed (or magnitude of the transverse velocity) is the magnitude of the cross product of the unit vector in the radial direction and the velocity vector

annotations.label: Angular momentum in scalar form

anchor.snippet: Angular momentum in scalar form is the mass times the distance to the origin times the transverse velocity

annotations.note: Angular momentum is a physics quantity not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Angular momentum in scalar form is the mass times the distance to the origin times the transverse velocity

annotations.label: Moment of inertia

anchor.snippet: is known as moment of inertia

annotations.note: Moment of inertia is a physics quantity with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Kepler's laws of planetary motion

anchor.snippet: These relations are known as Kepler's laws of planetary motion

annotations.note: A grep for `Kepler` found no declaration; Kepler's laws are not formalized in Mathlib.
